case_name: Jagdish Rai vs State Of Punjab And Others
court: High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh

compensation_breakdown.interest: 12% p.a. from date of filing the claim
compensation_breakdown.notes: Amount awarded by MACT and paid by State; recovery of Rs.83,200/- (1/3) from petitioner later directed but that recovery order was quashed by this High Court.

facts: The petitioner was a driver employed with the Assistant Excise and Taxation Commissioner, Muktsar. On 4.10.1991 a government Jeep (Reg. No. PAR-782) travelling from Fazilka to Ferozepur met with an accident causing the death of Constable Ranjit Singh; FIR No. 423 under Section 304 was registered and the petitioner was later acquitted in criminal trial. The Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awarded Rs. 2,49,600/- to the legal heirs of the deceased against respondents including the petitioner; the State paid the award and later issued a recovery order dated 26.11.1998 directing deduction of Rs. 83,200/- (1/3 share) from the petitioner's salary pursuant to a letter dated 7.9.1998. The petitioner challenged the recovery order by this writ petition.
arguments_claimant: The petitioner argued that the recovery directed from his salary was illegal and punitive and attracted the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970; therefore the mandatory procedure (notice, opportunity to reply) under Rule 10 must be followed before any recovery under Rule 5(iii) could be effected. He also pointed out the vehicle was not insured and invoked the State's obligation when exempted under Section 146 to create a specific fund; reliance was placed on Ajaib Singh v. State of Punjab for procedural requirements.
arguments_respondent: The State contended that the Tribunal's award under Section 166 read with Section 140 of the Motor Vehicles Act was binding on the petitioner and had been passed after giving him ample opportunity; accordingly, recovery pursuant to that award was not a disciplinary punishment requiring the procedural safeguards of the Rules. The State maintained that principles of natural justice need not be applied 'mechanically' at every stage.
ratio_decidendi: "An analysis of the above quoted rules would show that recovery from the pay of an employee of the whole or part of any pecuniary loss caused to the Government by negligence or breach of orders is one of the minor penalties"; and "no order imposing any of the penalties specified in Rule 5 (i) to (iv) could be passed except after: (a) informing the Government employee in writing of the proposal to take action against him and of the imputations of misconduct or misbehaviour on which it is proposed to be taken and giving him a reasonable opportunity of making such representation". The Court held that the procedure under Rule 10 of the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970 is mandatory before effecting recovery from pay as contemplated by Rule 5(iii). Because that procedure was admittedly not followed here, the recovery order dated 26.11.1998 was held to be invalid and was quashed, while leaving the respondents "at liberty to proceed in accordance with law and then pass a fresh order."
final_order: The writ petition is allowed and order dated 26.11.1998 (directing recovery of Rs. 83,200/- from the petitioner's salary) is quashed; respondents are at liberty to proceed in accordance with law and pass a fresh order.
summary: A government driver challenged an order directing recovery from his salary of one-third of a MACT award paid to the legal heirs of a person killed in a 1991 jeep accident. The High Court held that recovery from pay is a minor penalty under Rule 5(iii) of the Punjab Civil Service Rules and that the mandatory procedural requirements of Rule 10 (notice and opportunity to be heard) must be followed. Because those procedures were not followed, the recovery order was quashed. The MACT award of Rs. 2,49,600/- to the legal heirs remained intact.
embedding_summary: Jagdish Rai v. State of Punjab, High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh, CWP No. 6850 of 2001, decided April 22, 2009. Facts: government Jeep (Reg. PAR-782) on 4.10.1991 from Fazilka to Ferozepur met with an accident causing death of Constable Ranjit Singh; MACT awarded Rs. 2,49,600/- (with 12% p.a. interest) to legal heirs under Motor Vehicles Act; State paid award and then sought recovery of Rs. 83,200/- (1/3) from driver by order dated 26.11.1998 following letter dated 7.9.1998. Legal issues: vicarious liability of State, Section 146 MV Act exemption/fund obligation, binding nature of MACT awards under Section 166/Section 140, and whether recovery from pay is disciplinary invoking Rule 5(iii) and mandatory procedure under Rule 10 of the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970. Key precedents cited and relied upon include State of Maharashtra v. Kanchanmala Vijaysing Shirke (1995) 5 SCC 659; court held procedural safeguards required and quashed recovery order, leaving respondents free to proceed afresh. Outcome: recovery order quashed; MACT award Rs. 2,49,600/- preserved.
legal_principles: Recovery from pay is a minor penalty under Rule 5(iii) of the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970
Procedure in Rule 10 (notice and reasonable opportunity to make representation) is mandatory before imposing recovery under Rule 5(iii)
Vicarious liability of the State for acts of employees in course of employment should be approached liberally (per State of Maharashtra v. Kanchanmala)
MACT awards under Section 166/Section 140 of the Motor Vehicles Act are binding but recovery from an employee's pay to recoup such awards must follow statutory/procedural safeguards
A show cause/disciplinary procedure is required before effecting pecuniary recovery from a government employee's salary
sections_cited: Rule 5(iii) Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970
Rule 10 Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970
cases_cited: State of Maharashtra v. Kanchanmala Vijaysing Shirke, (1995) 5 SCC 659
Sahi Ram and another v. State of Haryana through Collector, District Mohindergarh and others, 1991 (Accident Claims Journal) (as cited in award)
The General Manager, Kerala State Road Transport Corporation, Trivandrum v. Mrs. Susamma Thomas and others (Supreme Court, decided 6-1-1993) (as cited in award)
Ajaib Singh v. State of Punjab, 2004 (4) RSJ 374
Hardip Singh (Division Bench of this Court) (citation not provided in text)
bm25_text: Jagdish Rai vs State Of Punjab And Others High Court of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh 2009 The petitioner was a driver employed with the Assistant Excise and Taxation Commissioner, Muktsar. On 4.10.1991 a government Jeep (Reg. No. PAR-782) travelling from Fazilka to Ferozepur met with an accident causing the death of Constable Ranjit Singh; FIR No. 423 under Section 304 was registered and the petitioner was later acquitted in criminal trial. The Motor Accident Claims Tribunal awarded Rs. 2,49,600/- to the legal heirs of the deceased against respondents including the petitioner; the State paid the award and later issued a recovery order dated 26.11.1998 directing deduction of Rs. 83,200/- (1/3 share) from the petitioner's salary pursuant to a letter dated 7.9.1998. The petitioner challenged the recovery order by this writ petition. The petitioner argued that the recovery directed from his salary was illegal and punitive and attracted the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970; therefore the mandatory procedure (notice, opportunity to reply) under Rule 10 must be followed before any recovery under Rule 5(iii) could be effected. He also pointed out the vehicle was not insured and invoked the State's obligation when exempted under Section 146 to create a specific fund; reliance was placed on Ajaib Singh v. State of Punjab for procedural requirements. The State contended that the Tribunal's award under Section 166 read with Section 140 of the Motor Vehicles Act was binding on the petitioner and had been passed after giving him ample opportunity; accordingly, recovery pursuant to that award was not a disciplinary punishment requiring the procedural safeguards of the Rules. The State maintained that principles of natural justice need not be applied 'mechanically' at every stage. "An analysis of the above quoted rules would show that recovery from the pay of an employee of the whole or part of any pecuniary loss caused to the Government by negligence or breach of orders is one of the minor penalties"; and "no order imposing any of the penalties specified in Rule 5 (i) to (iv) could be passed except after: (a) informing the Government employee in writing of the proposal to take action against him and of the imputations of misconduct or misbehaviour on which it is proposed to be taken and giving him a reasonable opportunity of making such representation". The Court held that the procedure under Rule 10 of the Punjab Civil Service (Punishment and Appeal) Rules, 1970 is mandatory before effecting recovery from pay as contemplated by Rule 5(iii). Because that procedure was admittedly not followed here, the recovery order dated 26.11.1998 was held to be invalid and was quashed, while leaving the respondents "at liberty to proceed in accordance with law and then pass a fresh order." The writ petition is allowed and order dated 26.11.1998 (directing recovery of Rs. 83,200/- from the petitioner's salary) is quashed; respondents are at liberty to proceed in accordance with law and pass a fresh order. A government driver challenged an order directing recovery from his salary of one-third of a MACT award paid to the legal heirs of a person killed in a 1991 jeep accident. The High Court held that recovery from pay is a minor penalty under Rule 5(iii) of the Punjab Civil Service Rules and that the mandatory procedural requirements of Rule 10 (notice and opportunity to be heard) must be followed. Because those procedures were not followed, the recovery order was quashed. The MACT award of Rs. 2,49,600/- to the legal heirs remained intact. Jagdish Rai v. Key precedents cited and relied upon include State of Maharashtra v.
